attributes.stem: Xenobiotics are exogenous chemicals that enter the body from environmental sources.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following best describes the sequential process of xenobiotic metabolism in the human body?
options: Phase I and phase II reactions occur simultaneously in all xenobiotic metabolism
Phase II reactions precede phase I reactions to facilitate excretion
Phase I reactions involve conjugation followed by phase II oxidation reactions
Phase I reactions involve hydrolysis, oxidation, or reduction followed by phase II conjugation reactions
Phase I reactions directly produce water-soluble compounds for immediate excretion
attributes.explanation:
The correct answer is that Phase I reactions involve hydrolysis, oxidation, or reduction followed by phase II conjugation reactions. Xenobiotic metabolism occurs in the liver and typically follows a sequential process where phase I reactions (primarily oxidation, reduction, or hydrolysis) modify the xenobiotic by introducing or exposing functional groups. These modified compounds then undergo phase II reactions, which are conjugation processes that attach polar groups to increase water solubility and facilitate excretion.

The incorrect options can be explained as follows:

  • Phase I reactions involve conjugation followed by phase II oxidation reactions - This reverses the actual sequence. Conjugation reactions are phase II reactions, not phase I, and they follow rather than precede oxidation reactions.

  • Phase II reactions precede phase I reactions to facilitate excretion - This incorrectly reverses the sequence. Phase I reactions typically occur first to create functional groups that can then be conjugated in phase II.

  • Phase I and phase II reactions occur simultaneously in all xenobiotic metabolism - While some overlap may occur in certain pathways, the general pattern is sequential processing with phase I preceding phase II.

  • Phase I reactions directly produce water-soluble compounds for immediate excretion - Phase I reactions often increase reactivity but typically do not sufficiently increase water solubility for efficient excretion. Phase II conjugation reactions are usually required to significantly enhance water solubility.

It's important to note that phase I reactions can either detoxify xenobiotics or, in some cases, bioactivate them to form more toxic intermediates. Phase II reactions generally increase water solubility through glucuronidation, sulfation, methylation, or conjugation with glutathione, facilitating elimination through urine or bile.
learningPoints: Xenobiotics are exogenous chemicals from the environment that enter the body through inhalation, ingestion, or skin contact.
Phase I reactions involve hydrolysis, oxidation, or reduction of xenobiotics.
Phase II reactions involve conjugation processes like glucuronidation, sulfation, methylation, and glutathione conjugation to increase water solubility.
Xenobiotic metabolism can result in either detoxification (creating inactive water-soluble products) or bioactivation (generating toxic metabolites).
references: Kumar V, Abbas AK, Aster JC. Robbins and Cotran Pathologic Basis of Disease. 10th ed. Philadelphia: Elsevier; 2020. Chapter 9; page 407-408.
